(emoji, rarer CJK characters in supplementary planes) saves
completely without silent truncation at the multi-byte boundary —
a classic symptom of a 3-byte UTF-8 column or naive byte-length
truncation.
preconditions: Part creation is available.

steps.action:
Create a part with description "Bracket assembly — premium 🔧
grade (𠮷野家 supplier)". Save.
steps.expected: Save succeeds. No characters are stripped or replaced.

steps.action: Reload the part. Read the description.
steps.expected:
Description matches input exactly, including the emoji and the
supplementary-plane CJK character (𠮷, U+20BB7).

steps.action: Edit the description to start with the emoji. Save and reload.
steps.expected:
Leading emoji is preserved. No truncation at the first byte
boundary.

steps.action:
Export the part list. Confirm the description in the export
matches.
steps.expected: Export round-trip is exact.

expected_overall: 4-byte UTF-8 characters are first-class in text fields.
pass_criteria:
Saved description matches input exactly AND emoji and supplementary-
plane characters survive round-trips.
why_this_matters:
Databases configured for 3-byte UTF-8 (the legacy MySQL default)
silently truncate 4-byte characters at the first one encountered.
The bug is invisible until a customer's emoji-laden part name comes
back blank.

title: A list of customers with accented names sorts by the configured database locale's collation, not by raw byte order
goal:
Verify that sorting a list of customers whose names mix accented
and unaccented Latin characters produces an order a human reader
would expect — "Ångström" near "Aalto," not at the end after "Z" —
per the collation rule defined by the configured database locale.
preconditions: At least 10 customers with names that include both accented and unaccented characters (e.g., "Aalto", "Ångström", "Bär", "Bärbel", "Cabo", "Café", "Zebra").
The deployment's database collation is configured and documented (default for this build: Postgres `und-x-icu` for general Unicode-aware ordering, or whatever locale the deploy chooses).

steps.action: Sort the customer list ascending by name.
steps.expected: Sort applies.

steps.action:
Inspect the order. Confirm "Ångström" appears near "Aalto" /
"Bär" — not after "Z" (which would indicate naive byte-order
sort treating Å as a high-codepoint character).
steps.expected:
Order matches a documented locale collation (e.g., en-US, where
Å is grouped with A; or Swedish locale, where Å sorts after Z).
The application's locale is documented somewhere accessible.

steps.action: Switch the user's locale (if supported) and re-sort.
steps.expected: Order changes per the new locale's collation rules.

expected_overall:
List sorting respects the collation rule defined by the configured
database locale, and that locale is documented in deployment docs.
pass_criteria:
Sort order matches the documented collation rule for the configured
locale AND no byte-order artifacts (Å, Ä, Ö dumped at end) appear
unless that is the configured locale's documented behavior.
why_this_matters:
Byte-order sorting puts every accented name at the end of the list,
which makes finding "Ångström" require scrolling past "Z." A real
ERP gets locale collation right.

title: A sales order line with quantity zero is either rejected or treated as informational with no GL impact
goal:
Verify that a sales order line entered with quantity = 0 either is
rejected at entry, or is permitted only as an informational marker
that does not post to revenue, AR, or inventory.
preconditions: At least one customer.
At least one part.

steps.action:
Create a sales order with one line at quantity = 0, unit price
$50.
steps.expected:
The application either:
(a) rejects the line with a "quantity must be positive" message,
OR
(b) accepts the line and clearly labels it as informational
(e.g., "no charge").
The behavior is documented.

steps.action: If accepted, attempt to ship and invoice. Inspect GL postings.
steps.expected:
No revenue, no AR, no inventory movement is generated by the
zero line. The order's net is unchanged.

steps.action: Run the order register / sales report.
steps.expected:
The zero-quantity line either does not appear, or appears with a
clear $0 marker that does not feed totals.

expected_overall: Zero-quantity lines do not corrupt revenue, AR, or inventory.
pass_criteria:
Behavior matches one of the documented policies AND no nonzero
posting results from a zero quantity.
why_this_matters:
A zero-quantity line that nonetheless triggers GL postings — even
zero-amount ones — clutters the audit trail and confuses
reconciliation. The system has to be deliberate about it.

title: A work order completion of zero good units does not credit production
goal:
Verify that posting a work order completion with zero good units
produced — typically because all output was scrap — does not credit
production output, that any scrapped quantity is recorded against
the proper scrap account, and that material issued is not
inadvertently "returned" by the zero completion.
preconditions: At least one open work order with materials issued.

steps.action:
Post a completion for the work order with: good = 0, scrap = 5
(the entire run was scrap).
steps.expected:
The application accepts the completion with explicit zero good
and a positive scrap. OR: it rejects "zero good with positive
scrap" requiring a separate scrap-only path. Either policy is
documented.

steps.action: Inspect the GL postings.
steps.expected:
No credit to FG inventory or production output for the zero
good. The scrap quantity posts to a documented scrap / variance
account.

steps.action:
Verify materials issued are NOT silently returned to raw stock
by the zero completion.
steps.expected: Material issuance remains; only the explicit scrap is recorded.

steps.action: Run the WO variance report.
steps.expected:
WO shows full material issuance, zero output, and a variance
attributable to the all-scrap run.

expected_overall:
Zero-good completions handle scrap explicitly and never silently
reverse material issuance.
pass_criteria:
No FG credit AND material issuance preserved AND scrap posts to
documented account AND WO variance reflects the loss.
why_this_matters:
All-scrap runs happen in real production. The system has to handle
them as a recognizable economic event, not as a no-op or a silent
material reversal.

title: A $0 customer invoice (warranty replacement) records the shipment without inflating AR
goal:
Verify that a customer invoice intentionally totaling $0 — for
example, a warranty replacement where the customer is not charged —
records the shipment in inventory and on the customer's transaction
history without creating a $0 open AR row that clutters aging.
preconditions: At least one customer with at least one shipped item.
A workflow path for warranty / no-charge shipment exists, OR a standard invoice can be issued at $0.

steps.action: Create a customer invoice with one line: 1 unit at $0.
steps.expected:
The application accepts the $0 invoice OR routes it to a
"no-charge" document type. Either way, the document is
auditable.

steps.action:
Inspect inventory: the 1 unit shipped depleted on-hand
correctly.
steps.expected: Inventory reflects the shipment.

steps.action: Inspect AR aging.
steps.expected:
The $0 invoice either does NOT appear in AR aging at all (it has
no balance), OR appears with $0 in the right age bucket and is
filtered out of aging totals. No phantom open balance accrues.

steps.action: Inspect GL postings.
steps.expected:
Cost of goods sold posts (the inventory left the building);
revenue posts $0; AR posts $0. No imbalance.

expected_overall:
Zero-dollar shipments record economic reality (COGS, inventory)
without polluting AR aging.
pass_criteria:
Inventory depleted AND COGS posted AND AR aging not cluttered with
$0 open rows AND GL balanced.
why_this_matters:
Warranty and sample shipments are real cost events even when no
invoice is charged. The system has to handle them without forcing
the user to either fake-charge or skip the GL impact.

title: Posted financials sync to the connected accounting system
goal:
Verify that with the accounting integration set to "connected"
(P0-INTEG-001), each posted financial transaction (invoice, JE,
payment) creates a corresponding record in the external accounting
system within the documented sync window.
preconditions: The accounting integration is configured to a test sandbox (not real).
At least one customer invoice has been posted in the period.

steps.action:
Post a customer invoice (or use one already posted). Open the
sync status / queue.
steps.expected: The invoice appears in the queue, status "Pending sync."

steps.action: Wait for the next sync (or trigger manually).
steps.expected:
Invoice transitions to "Synced." Sync log captures the external
ID.

steps.action:
Open the external accounting sandbox. Find the corresponding
record.
steps.expected:
Record exists with matching customer, amount, date, and
reference.

steps.action:
Force a sync failure (e.g., temporarily revoke the credential).
Post another transaction.
steps.expected:
Failure is captured in the sync log with a clear reason. The
transaction stays "Pending" or "Failed" but is NOT silently
dropped.

expected_overall: Outbound accounting sync is reliable and surfaces failures.
pass_criteria:
Successful sync captures external ID AND failure is visible AND
no silent loss.

title: Outbound EDI 810 invoice dispatched to a customer
goal:
Verify that posting a customer invoice to an EDI customer
generates and sends an 810 with line / pricing / tax / terms
matching the posted invoice.
preconditions: A trading partner expects 810.
A posted customer invoice for that customer exists.

steps.action: Post an invoice to an EDI-configured customer.
steps.expected: 810 is generated and queued / dispatched.

steps.action:
Inspect the 810. Verify lines, totals, taxes, terms, customer
PO, ship-to / bill-to all match the posted invoice.
steps.expected: Match.

steps.action:
Track delivery / acknowledgment from the trading partner (a
997 functional ack).
steps.expected:
997 is received and logged. The invoice's EDI status updates
to "Acknowledged."

expected_overall: 810 dispatch and acknowledgment loop work end-to-end.
pass_criteria:
810 dispatched AND fields tie to invoice AND 997 acknowledged
status visible.

title: Inbound EDI 850 from a customer creates a sales order
goal:
Verify the application can ingest an EDI 850 (PO from a customer)
and create a corresponding sales order with correct customer,
line items, prices, and ship-to / bill-to.
preconditions: The application supports EDI inbound (or has a test trading partner configured).
At least one customer is set up for EDI.

steps.action:
Submit a sample EDI 850 file (with customer / part numbers
mapped per the trading partner's specification).
steps.expected:
File is ingested. Sales order is created in draft (or open)
status.

steps.action:
Open the SO. Verify customer, lines, prices, ship-to, customer
PO, and date all came through.
steps.expected: All fields match the 850.

steps.action: Submit a malformed 850 (bad segment).
steps.expected:
File is rejected. Error is logged. SO is NOT created. The user
can see what's wrong.

expected_overall:
EDI 850 inbound creates SOs cleanly with error visibility on
malformed input.
pass_criteria:
Valid 850 → SO with matching fields AND malformed 850 produces a
clear error.

title: Outbound EDI 855 acknowledges an incoming customer PO
goal:
Verify that after ingesting an 850 (or accepting a customer PO),
the application generates and dispatches a corresponding 855
(PO acknowledgment) with the right approval / change codes.
preconditions: At least one inbound 850 has produced an SO (per INTEG-EDI-850-001).

steps.action: Confirm or accept the SO derived from an inbound 850.
steps.expected: An 855 generation action is available (or auto-fires).

steps.action: Generate / send the 855.
steps.expected:
855 is dispatched to the trading partner. The application logs
the send.

steps.action: Modify a line price on the SO. Re-send 855.
steps.expected:
Updated 855 reflects the price change with the appropriate
change code, NOT silently confirming the original.

expected_overall: 855 reflects the SO's actual state with proper change codes.
pass_criteria:
855 dispatched AND fields match SO AND change reflected on
resend.

title: Outbound EDI 856 ASN dispatched at shipment
goal:
Verify that when a shipment goes out (P4-SHIP), the application
generates and sends an 856 ASN to the customer with the correct
packing structure (pack-level, item-level, serial-level if
applicable).
preconditions: A trading partner expects 856.
A shipment is ready to send.

steps.action: Ship a packed order to an EDI customer.
steps.expected:
856 is generated. The application's send log shows the
dispatch.

steps.action: Inspect the generated 856 (raw or via a test partner).
steps.expected:
Pack hierarchy is correct (shipment → packs → cartons →
items). Tracking number, ship date, and ship-to are present.

steps.action: For a serialized product, verify the 856 includes serials.
steps.expected: Serials present at item level.

expected_overall:
856 dispatch is correct for both serialized and non-serialized
products.
pass_criteria:
856 generated AND fields complete AND serials included where
applicable.

title: Export a list to CSV / Excel and re-import to verify the round-trip
goal:
Verify that exporting an entity list to CSV / Excel produces a file
that can be re-imported (after appropriate edits) without breaking
references or losing data fidelity.
preconditions: At least 20 records of one entity exist.

steps.action: Open the entity list. Export all to CSV.
steps.expected: File downloads. Header row matches the import template.

steps.action:
Open the file. Verify the row count equals the source count
(no truncation).
steps.expected: Match.

steps.action: Edit a non-key field on 5 rows. Save. Re-import.
steps.expected:
Re-import detects the rows as updates (not duplicates),
updates the modified field, and reports the update count.

expected_overall: File round-trips cleanly.
pass_criteria:
Exported = source AND re-import updates correctly AND no
duplicates created.

title: Generic file-based import works with row-level error reporting
goal:
Verify file-based import for any importable entity (parts,
vendors, customers, GL accounts) works with documented templates,
flags row errors with line and column detail, and never silently
drops bad rows or partial outcomes.
preconditions: At least one importable entity has a documented CSV / Excel template.

steps.action:
Pick an importable entity. Download its template. Fill in 10
rows: 8 valid, 2 with errors of different types (e.g., missing
required field, invalid foreign key, duplicate key).
steps.expected: Template is clear about required vs. optional fields.

steps.action: Upload the file.
steps.expected:
Validation summary shows 8 rows ready, 2 errored. Each error
gives line number, column, and reason.

steps.action: Confirm import.
steps.expected:
8 records created. The 2 bad rows are NOT silently skipped —
they appear in an error report the user can fix and re-upload.

expected_overall: File-based import works with row-level error visibility.
pass_criteria:
Valid rows imported AND bad rows reported AND no silent partial
outcomes.

title: BOM list supports partial-match search by parent part / description
goal:
Verify the BOM list search is partial, case-insensitive, and
matches against the parent part number and BOM / part description.
preconditions: At least 50 BOMs exist across multiple parent parts and revisions.

steps.action: Open the BOM list. Search for a partial parent part number.
steps.expected:
Partial match returns BOMs whose parent part number contains
that fragment (case-insensitive).

steps.action: Search for a partial description fragment.
steps.expected: BOMs whose parent description contains the fragment appear.

steps.action: Search for a string that does not match any BOM.
steps.expected: Empty-state message shown. No stale rows.

steps.action: Combine search with a status filter (e.g., Active).
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

expected_overall:
BOM list search supports the engineering "find that BOM" workflow
without requiring exact identifiers.
pass_criteria: Partial / case-insensitive / multi-field search works correctly.

title: BOM list filters by status, revision, and effectivity date
goal:
Verify the BOM list supports filtering by status (draft / active /
obsolete), by revision (latest only vs all revisions), and by
effectivity date (BOMs effective on a given date).
preconditions: BOMs exist with multiple revisions per parent and a mix of statuses and effectivity windows.

steps.action: Filter to status = Active. Latest revision only.
steps.expected:
Only the latest active revision per parent appears. Older
revisions hidden.

steps.action: Toggle "show all revisions".
steps.expected:
All revisions appear, including superseded ones, clearly
labeled with their revision number.

steps.action: Filter to BOMs effective on a specific past date.
steps.expected:
Result is the BOMs whose effectivity window contained that
date — including ones now superseded.

steps.action: Combine effectivity filter with status = Obsolete.
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

steps.action: Clear all filters.
steps.expected: Default view restored.

expected_overall:
BOM filtering supports both current-engineering and historical
"what was effective then" queries.
pass_criteria: Status / revision / effectivity filters return correct sets.

title: BOM list supports stable multi-column sort
goal:
Verify the BOM list supports multi-column sort (e.g., parent
part, then revision, then effectivity date) with stable ordering.
preconditions: At least 50 BOMs exist with multiple revisions per parent.

steps.action: Sort by parent part number (ascending) as primary.
steps.expected: BOMs group by parent part.

steps.action: Add secondary sort: revision (descending).
steps.expected: Within each parent, latest revision listed first.

steps.action: Add tertiary sort: effectivity start date (descending).
steps.expected: Ties within (parent, revision) break by effectivity date.

steps.action: Reverse primary direction.
steps.expected: Group order flips. Secondary and tertiary unchanged.

steps.action: Clear all sorts.
steps.expected: Default ordering restored.

expected_overall: Multi-column sort supports realistic engineering review views.
pass_criteria: Sort precedence is correct, stable, and visually communicated.

title: BOM list paginates cleanly over a 5,000+ row dataset
goal:
Verify the BOM list scales to a realistic engineering catalog
(>= 5,000 BOMs counting all revisions) without slow loads or
pagination errors.
preconditions: Test environment must have >= 5,000 rows of the entity under test seeded; use `/api/v1/dev/seed-bulk-list?entity=bom&count=5000` (when available) or pre-seed via DB script.
At least 5,000 BOM records exist (across parents and revisions).

steps.action: Open the BOM list. Note total record count.
steps.expected:
Total reflects all BOM records. Initial page renders in under
2 seconds.

steps.action: Page forward several times, then jump to last.
steps.expected: Each page is correct. No duplicates or skipped rows.

steps.action:
Toggle "latest revision only" and verify pagination on the
reduced set.
steps.expected: Pagination recalculates correctly.

steps.action: Change page size to the largest supported option.
steps.expected: List re-paginates correctly.

expected_overall: BOM list is usable at production catalog scale.
pass_criteria: Pagination correctness and responsiveness hold at 5,000+ rows.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — depends on dev seed-helper endpoint per
L3 in phase-2-library-reconciliation.md.

title: BOM list exports the currently visible rows and columns
goal:
Verify the BOM list export honors active filter, sort, and column
visibility, and that revision and effectivity dates export with
correct types.
preconditions: At least 50 BOMs exist across statuses and revisions.

steps.action:
Filter to status = Active, latest revision only. Sort by
parent part ascending. Hide one column.
steps.expected: On-screen view reflects all settings.

steps.action: Trigger CSV export.
steps.expected: File downloads with sensible filename.

steps.action:
Open in spreadsheet tool. Verify row count and column set
match the on-screen view.
steps.expected:
Match. Hidden column absent. Effectivity dates exported as
dates, not text.

steps.action:
Verify revision column exports with the same revision label
shown on screen.
steps.expected: Revision label preserved exactly.

expected_overall: BOM export is suitable for engineering review and external sharing.
pass_criteria: Filter / sort / columns honored. Date and revision types preserved.

title: Customer list supports search, filter, sort, pagination, and persisted table state
goal:
Verify the customer list view: partial-match search by name and ID,
filter by status / region / credit band, sort on every column,
pagination over a non-trivial dataset, and persisting / recalling
table state per-user via any mechanism (either a dedicated
saved-views UI affordance or a generic key/value preference store).
preconditions: At least 50 customers exist (for filter / sort / paginate coverage). Some active, some inactive.

steps.action:
Open the customer list. Type a partial customer name (e.g.,
"ACM") into search.
steps.expected:
Results filter live to matching customers (case-insensitive,
partial match).

steps.action: Apply a filter: status = Inactive.
steps.expected: Only inactive customers shown.

steps.action: Sort by name (ascending), then by created date (descending).
steps.expected: Sort applied. Results reflect sort order.

steps.action: Paginate to last page.
steps.expected: Last page returns the expected tail of the sorted set.

steps.action:
Persist the current filter / sort under a name / identifier via
whatever mechanism the application provides — dedicated
saved-views menu, or a generic user-preferences key/value entry
(e.g., POST to /user-preferences). Recall it.
steps.expected:
Configuration is stored against the current user. If a
saved-views UI exists, the entry appears in the menu;
otherwise the preference persists in the underlying store.
Recalling restores filters.

expected_overall:
Customer list is fully usable for normal-day workflows, including
persisted table state regardless of whether the application surfaces
it as a first-class saved-views UI or a generic user-preferences
key/value store.
pass_criteria:
Search / filter / sort / paginate all work correctly AND persist /
recall of table state works cleanly. Either a dedicated UI affordance
OR a generic key/value preference store satisfies the persistence
requirement.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — when the application develops a
dedicated saved-views UI, this case will tighten to require
that surface.

title: Customer list paginates cleanly over a 5,000+ row dataset
goal:
Verify the customer list remains responsive and accurate when the
dataset exceeds 5,000 rows. Page size, total count, page jump, and
last-page behavior all work without silent truncation.
preconditions: Test environment must have >= 5,000 rows of the entity under test seeded; use `/api/v1/dev/seed-bulk-list?entity=customer&count=5000` (when available) or pre-seed via DB script.
At least 5,000 customers exist in the dataset (use bulk import or seeded data if needed).

steps.action:
Open the customer list. Note the displayed total record count
and the page size selector value.
steps.expected:
Total count reflects the full dataset (>= 5,000). Page size
selector exposes a sane range (e.g., 25 / 50 / 100).

steps.action: Page through the first three pages using next-page control.
steps.expected:
Each page loads in under 2 seconds. No duplicate or missing
rows across page boundaries.

steps.action: Jump directly to the last page (or enter the last page number).
steps.expected:
Last page renders the final partial set. Row count on last page
equals total mod page size (or full page if evenly divisible).

steps.action: Change page size to the largest available option and reload.
steps.expected: List re-paginates correctly. Total page count updates.

steps.action:
Apply a filter that should reduce the set to under one page,
then clear the filter.
steps.expected:
Pagination collapses to one page under the filter, then
restores to the full pagination footprint when cleared.

expected_overall:
Customer list is usable at production scale with no silent
truncation.
pass_criteria:
Total count, page navigation, page size changes, and filter
interaction with pagination all behave correctly.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — depends on dev seed-helper endpoint per
L3 in phase-2-library-reconciliation.md.

title: Customer list supports date-range filter (created / last activity)
goal:
Verify the customer list supports date-range filtering on at least
two date fields (created date, last activity / last order date)
and handles open-ended ranges, single-day ranges, and inverted
ranges sensibly.
preconditions: Customers exist with created dates spanning the last 24 months and varied last-activity dates.

steps.action:
Open the customer list. Apply a date-range filter on created
date covering the last 30 days.
steps.expected: Only customers created in that window appear.

steps.action: Apply a single-day range (start = end) on a known busy day.
steps.expected: Only customers created on that day appear.

steps.action: Apply an open-ended range (start only, no end).
steps.expected: All customers created on or after the start date appear.

steps.action:
Switch the filter field to last-activity date and apply a
90-day window.
steps.expected: Result reflects last-activity, not created date.

steps.action: Enter an inverted range (end before start).
steps.expected:
System rejects with a clear validation message, or normalizes
the range. Either is acceptable; silent empty result is not.

expected_overall: Date-range filtering is usable for typical AR / CRM workflows.
pass_criteria:
Each date filter scenario returns the correct set. Inverted range
is handled with a clear signal.

title: Customer list columns can be shown, hidden, and reordered
goal:
Verify the customer list lets the user choose which columns are
visible and (where supported) reorder them, and that the choice
persists per-user across reloads.
preconditions: Customer list view is open with the default column set.

steps.action:
Open the column chooser. Hide three default columns and show
at least one optional column not in the default set.
steps.expected:
List redraws with the selected columns. Hidden columns are
gone; optional column appears.

steps.action:
Reorder columns by dragging (or via the chooser's order
controls).
steps.expected: Column order updates in the list.

steps.action: Reload the page (or sign out and back in).
steps.expected: Column visibility and order persist for this user.

steps.action: Reset to default columns.
steps.expected: Default column set is restored.

steps.action:
Confirm that hiding a column does not affect filter / sort
capability on that column (filter / sort menu still lists it).
steps.expected: Hidden columns remain available as filter / sort targets.

expected_overall:
Users can tailor the list to their workflow without losing access
to underlying fields.
pass_criteria:
Show / hide / reorder works, persists per-user, and does not
silently disable filtering or sorting.

title: Customer list exports the currently visible rows and columns
goal:
Verify the customer list export honors the active filter, sort,
and column visibility choices, and produces a usable file (CSV
and / or XLSX) that opens cleanly in standard spreadsheet tools.
preconditions: At least 100 customers exist. User has permission to export.

steps.action:
Apply a filter (e.g., status = Active, region = West) and a
sort (name ascending). Hide a column.
steps.expected: List shows the filtered, sorted, reduced-column view.

steps.action: Trigger export (CSV).
steps.expected: File downloads. Filename suggests entity and timestamp.

steps.action:
Open the file in a spreadsheet tool. Verify row count, column
set, and order match the on-screen view.
steps.expected:
Exported rows match the filtered set. Hidden column is absent.
Column order matches on-screen order. Sort order is preserved.

steps.action: Repeat with XLSX export if offered.
steps.expected:
XLSX file opens cleanly. Numeric / date types preserved (not
stringified).

steps.action: Export with no filter applied (full set).
steps.expected:
Full export completes. If row cap exists, system warns the
user before silently truncating.

expected_overall:
Export reflects what the user sees on screen and is suitable for
downstream analysis.
pass_criteria:
Filter / sort / column visibility all honored in the export. No
silent truncation.

title: Employee list supports partial-match search by name / employee ID
goal:
Verify the employee list search is partial, case-insensitive, and
matches against employee name (first / last), employee ID / badge
number, and email.
preconditions: At least 100 employees exist with varied names and badge / ID formats.

steps.action: Open the employee list. Search for a partial last name.
steps.expected:
Partial match returns employees whose last name contains the
fragment (case-insensitive).

steps.action: Search for a partial first name.
steps.expected: Employees whose first name contains the fragment appear.

steps.action: Search for a partial employee ID / badge number.
steps.expected: Employees with that ID fragment appear.

steps.action: Search for a partial email address.
steps.expected: Employees whose email contains the fragment appear.

steps.action: Combine search with a status filter (e.g., Active).
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

expected_overall:
Employee search supports the day-to-day "find that person"
workflow.
pass_criteria: Partial / case-insensitive / multi-field search works correctly.

title: Employee list filters by department, employment status, and type
goal:
Verify the employee list filters by department, employment status
(active / on-leave / terminated), and employment type (full-time /
part-time / contractor), with combinations supported.
preconditions: Employees exist across at least three departments, all employment statuses, and at least two employment types.

steps.action: Open the employee list with default view.
steps.expected:
Default view shows active employees. Terminated excluded
unless explicitly opted in.

steps.action: Filter to department = Manufacturing.
steps.expected: Only Manufacturing employees appear.

steps.action: Multi-select department: Manufacturing + Engineering.
steps.expected: Result is the union.

steps.action: Combine department filter with employment type = Contractor.
steps.expected: Result is the intersection — contractors in those departments.

steps.action:
Switch status filter to include Terminated and confirm the
list expands.
steps.expected:
Terminated employees now visible. Default view choice does
not silently leak across saved-view boundaries.

expected_overall:
Department / status / type filtering supports HR and
administration workflows.
pass_criteria:
Each filter and combination returns the correct subset. Default
view excludes terminated unless opted in.

title: Employee list supports stable multi-column sort
goal:
Verify the employee list supports multi-column sort (e.g.,
department, then last name, then hire date) with stable ordering
and visible precedence.
preconditions: At least 50 employees exist across multiple departments and hire dates.

steps.action: Sort by department (ascending) as primary.
steps.expected: Employees group by department alphabetically.

steps.action: Add secondary sort: last name (ascending).
steps.expected: Within each department, employees alphabetized by last name.

steps.action: Add tertiary sort: hire date (ascending).
steps.expected:
Ties within (department, last name) break by earliest hire
date first.

steps.action: Reverse primary direction.
steps.expected:
Department group order flips. Secondary and tertiary
unchanged.

steps.action: Clear all sorts.
steps.expected: Default ordering restored.

expected_overall: Multi-column sort supports realistic HR / admin views.
pass_criteria: Sort precedence is correct, stable, and visually communicated.

title: Employee list paginates cleanly over a 5,000+ row dataset
goal:
Verify the employee list scales to a large workforce (>= 5,000
employees, including terminated history) without slow loads or
pagination errors.
preconditions: Test environment must have >= 5,000 rows of the entity under test seeded; use `/api/v1/dev/seed-bulk-list?entity=employee&count=5000` (when available) or pre-seed via DB script.
At least 5,000 employee records exist (active + terminated history).

steps.action:
Open the employee list with "include terminated" enabled. Note
total count.
steps.expected:
Total reflects all employee records. Initial page renders in
under 2 seconds.

steps.action: Page forward several times, then jump to last.
steps.expected: Each page is correct. No duplicates or skipped rows.

steps.action:
Filter to status = Active and verify pagination on the
reduced set.
steps.expected: Pagination recalculates correctly.

steps.action: Change page size to the largest supported option.
steps.expected: List re-paginates correctly.

expected_overall: Employee list is usable at large-employer scale.
pass_criteria: Pagination correctness and responsiveness hold at 5,000+ rows.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — depends on dev seed-helper endpoint per
L3 in phase-2-library-reconciliation.md.

title: Employee list columns can be shown, hidden, and reordered
goal:
Verify the employee list lets the user tailor visible columns
(e.g., hide salary, show shift / supervisor / hire date) and that
the choice persists per-user. Sensitive columns respect
permissions even when shown.
preconditions: Employee list view is open. Test user has permission to view the columns being toggled.

steps.action:
Open the column chooser. Hide one default column and show
shift, supervisor, and hire date.
steps.expected: List redraws with the chosen columns.

steps.action: Reorder columns so name is first, department is second.
steps.expected: Column order updates.

steps.action: Reload the page.
steps.expected: Column visibility and order persist for this user.

steps.action:
As a non-HR user, attempt to show a sensitive column (e.g.,
salary).
steps.expected:
Sensitive column is either not offered in the chooser, or is
shown masked. No leak of sensitive data.

steps.action: Reset columns to default.
steps.expected: Default column set restored.

expected_overall:
Column tailoring is usable for HR / admin workflows and respects
data-sensitivity boundaries.
pass_criteria:
Show / hide / reorder works, persists per-user, and sensitive
columns are gated by permissions.

title: Invoice list supports filter by customer, status, aging, and sort
goal:
Verify the customer invoice list supports filter by customer,
status (draft / sent / paid / partial / void), aging bucket, and
sort on every column.
preconditions: At least 20 customer invoices exist across statuses and aging buckets.

steps.action:
Open the invoice list. Filter to status = Open + aging > 30
days.
steps.expected: Result matches.

steps.action: Sort by amount due (descending).
steps.expected: Sort applied.

steps.action: Search by customer PO number.
steps.expected: Match works.

steps.expected:
Result is open invoices due in that window — a typical

steps.action: Enter an inverted range and confirm clear handling.
steps.expected:
System rejects with validation, or normalizes. Silent empty
result is not acceptable.

pass_criteria: Each scenario returns the correct invoice subset.

title: Invoice list supports stable multi-column sort
goal:
Verify the invoice list supports multi-column sort (e.g., status,
then due date, then amount) with stable ordering and visible
precedence.
preconditions: At least 50 invoices exist across statuses, due dates, and amounts.

steps.action: Sort by status (ascending) as primary.
steps.expected: Invoices group by status.

steps.action: Add secondary sort: due date (ascending).
steps.expected: Within each status, invoices are ordered earliest-due first.

steps.action: Add tertiary sort: amount due (descending).
steps.expected:
Within each (status, due date) group, ties break by amount
largest-first.

steps.expected: Group order flips. Secondary and tertiary unchanged.

steps.action: Clear all sorts.
steps.expected: Default ordering restored.

expected_overall: Multi-column sort supports collections / AR triage views.
pass_criteria: Sort precedence is correct, stable, and visually communicated.

title: Part list supports persisting and recalling table state per-user
goal:
Verify the parts list supports persisting and recalling table
state (filter / sort / column configuration) per-user via any
mechanism — either a dedicated saved-views UI affordance or a
generic key/value preference store. The persistence must allow
saving a configuration under an identifier, recalling it later,
updating it, and removing it cleanly.
preconditions: At least 100 parts exist across multiple types and statuses.

steps.action:
Configure the list: filter type = Raw material, status =
Active, sort by part number ascending, hide cost column.
steps.expected: List reflects the configuration.

steps.action:
Persist the configuration under a name / identifier (e.g.,
"My active raw materials") via whatever mechanism the
application provides — dedicated saved-views menu, or a
generic user-preferences key/value entry (e.g., POST to
/user-preferences).

Switch to a different list configuration, then recall the
persisted configuration via the same mechanism used in step 2.
steps.expected:
Recalled configuration restores the exact filter / sort /
column set.

steps.action:
Update the persisted configuration: change one filter and
save in place under the same identifier.
steps.expected:
Stored configuration updates. Re-recalling loads the new
config.

steps.action: Delete the persisted configuration.
steps.expected:
Entry removed from the persistence layer (menu or key/value
store). Default list view unaffected.

expected_overall:
Persisted table state supports recurring engineering /
procurement workflows regardless of whether the application
surfaces it as a first-class saved-views UI or as a generic
user-preferences key/value store.
pass_criteria:
Persist, recall, update, and delete of table state all work
cleanly. State is scoped per-user (or per-team if shared, with
clear ownership). Either a dedicated UI affordance OR a generic
key/value preference store satisfies this requirement.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — when the application develops a
dedicated saved-views UI, this case will tighten to require
that surface.

title: Vendor list paginates cleanly over a 5,000+ row dataset
goal:
Verify the vendor list handles a large dataset (>= 5,000 vendors)
without slow loads, missing rows, or broken pagination footers.
preconditions: Test environment must have >= 5,000 rows of the entity under test seeded; use `/api/v1/dev/seed-bulk-list?entity=vendor&count=5000` (when available) or pre-seed via DB script.
At least 5,000 vendors exist (seeded or imported).

steps.action: Open the vendor list and note total record count.
steps.expected:
Total reflects the full vendor count. Initial page renders in
under 2 seconds.

steps.action: Navigate forward several pages, then jump to the last page.
steps.expected:
Each page renders cleanly. No duplicate or skipped rows. Last
page row count matches the expected remainder.

steps.action: Change page size to the largest supported option.
steps.expected: Pagination footer updates correctly. List remains responsive.

steps.action:
Apply a partial-name search that should match a few hundred
vendors and verify pagination across the filtered set.
steps.expected:
Pagination recalculates against the filtered total. No silent
truncation of the filtered set.

expected_overall: Vendor list is usable at production scale.
pass_criteria: Pagination correctness and responsiveness hold at 5,000+ rows.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — depends on dev seed-helper endpoint per
L3 in phase-2-library-reconciliation.md.

title: Vendor list supports date-range filter (last PO / onboarded)
goal:
Verify the vendor list supports date-range filtering on at least
two date fields (vendor onboarded date, last PO date) and produces
a defensible result for procurement triage.
preconditions: Vendors exist with onboarded dates and last-PO dates spanning at least the last 24 months.

steps.action:
Open the vendor list. Apply a date-range filter on
onboarded-date covering the last 12 months.
steps.expected: Only vendors onboarded in that window appear.

steps.action:
Switch the filter field to last-PO-date and apply a 90-day
window.
steps.expected:
Result is the set of vendors with at least one PO in that
window. Vendors with no recent PO are excluded.

steps.action: Combine the date filter with status = Active.
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

steps.action: Apply an open-ended range (start only). Then clear the filter.
steps.expected: Open-ended filter works. Clearing returns to the full list.

expected_overall:
Date-range filtering supports common vendor-management triage
questions.
pass_criteria: Each date scenario returns the correct vendor subset.

title: Vendor list supports stable multi-column sort
goal:
Verify the vendor list supports sorting on multiple columns at
once with stable ordering, and that the sort indicator UI clearly
shows precedence.
preconditions: At least 50 vendors exist with overlapping values in at least two sortable columns (e.g., several vendors share the same country, several share the same currency).

steps.action: Sort by country (ascending) as the primary sort.
steps.expected:
Vendors group by country alphabetically. Sort indicator shows
country as primary.

steps.action: Add a secondary sort: vendor name (ascending).
steps.expected:
Within each country group, vendors are alphabetized by name.
Sort indicator shows the precedence (country, then name).

steps.action: Add a tertiary sort: created date (descending).
steps.expected:
Within each (country, name) group, ties break by created date
newest-first. Indicator shows the three-level precedence.

steps.action: Reverse the primary sort direction (country descending).
steps.expected: Group order flips. Secondary and tertiary sorts unchanged.

steps.action: Clear all sorts.
steps.expected: List returns to default ordering. Sort indicators clear.

expected_overall: Multi-column sort is reliable and the precedence is obvious.
pass_criteria: Sort precedence is correct, stable, and visually communicated.

title: Vendor list exports the currently visible rows and columns
goal:
Verify the vendor list export honors the active filter, sort, and
column visibility, and that 1099 / tax fields export correctly.
preconditions: At least 50 vendors exist with mixed 1099 status and currencies.

steps.action:
Filter to 1099-eligible. Sort by YTD spend descending. Hide
one column.
steps.expected: On-screen view reflects all three settings.

steps.action: Trigger CSV export.
steps.expected: File downloads with a sensible filename.

steps.action:
Open in a spreadsheet tool. Verify row count, column set,
column order, and sort match the on-screen view.
steps.expected:
Match. Hidden column absent. Numeric YTD spend exports as a
number, not a string.

steps.action:
Verify TIN / tax-ID column (if visible and exportable) is
formatted correctly and not silently masked.
steps.expected:
TIN format preserved. If masked on screen, masking decision in
the export is consistent with permissions.

expected_overall: Export is reliable for procurement / 1099 reporting prep.
pass_criteria:
Filter / sort / columns honored. Numeric and tax-ID fields
exported with correct types.

title: WO list supports filter by status, work center, due date, and sort
goal:
Verify the WO list view filters by status (planned / released /
in-progress / complete / closed), by work center, by due date
range, and sorts on every column including computed (% complete,
variance).
preconditions: At least 30 WOs exist across multiple statuses.

steps.action:
Open the WO list. Filter to "in-progress" + work center =
Press Shop.
steps.expected: Result matches both filters.

steps.action: Add a date range: due in next 7 days.
steps.expected: Result narrows.

steps.action: Sort by due date (ascending).
steps.expected: Earliest due first.

steps.action: Search by WO number (partial).
steps.expected: Partial match works.

expected_overall: WO list is usable for daily floor management.
pass_criteria: Filters / sort / search all work correctly.

title: WO list paginates cleanly over a 5,000+ row dataset
goal:
Verify the WO list scales to a year of production history
(>= 5,000 WOs) without slow loads or pagination errors.
preconditions: Test environment must have >= 5,000 rows of the entity under test seeded; use `/api/v1/dev/seed-bulk-list?entity=wo&count=5000` (when available) or pre-seed via DB script.
At least 5,000 WOs exist across statuses, work centers, and due dates.

steps.action: Open the WO list. Note total record count.
steps.expected:
Total reflects full WO history. Initial page renders in under
2 seconds.

steps.action: Page through forward several times, then jump to last page.
steps.expected:
Each page is correct. No duplicate or skipped rows across
boundaries.

steps.action:
Filter to status = In-progress and verify pagination on the
filtered set.
steps.expected: Pagination recalculates against filtered total.

steps.action: Change page size to the largest supported option.
steps.expected: List re-paginates correctly.

expected_overall: WO list is usable at production-floor scale.
pass_criteria: Pagination correctness and responsiveness hold at 5,000+ rows.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — depends on dev seed-helper endpoint per
L3 in phase-2-library-reconciliation.md.

title: WO list supports stable multi-column sort
goal:
Verify the WO list supports a multi-column sort (e.g., work
center, then due date, then priority) with stable ordering.
preconditions: At least 50 WOs exist across multiple work centers, due dates, and priorities.

steps.action: Sort by work center (ascending) as primary.
steps.expected: WOs group by work center alphabetically.

steps.action: Add secondary sort: due date (ascending).
steps.expected: Within each work center, WOs are ordered earliest-due first.

steps.action: Add tertiary sort: priority (descending, high-first).
steps.expected:
Within each (work center, due date) group, ties break by
priority high-first.

steps.action: Reverse primary direction.
steps.expected: Group order flips. Secondary and tertiary unchanged.

steps.action: Clear all sorts.
steps.expected: Default ordering restored.

expected_overall: Multi-column sort supports realistic floor-management views.
pass_criteria: Sort precedence is correct, stable, and visually communicated.

title: WO list supports persisting and recalling table state per-user
goal:
Verify the WO list supports persisting and recalling table state
per-user via any mechanism — either a dedicated saved-views UI
affordance or a generic key/value preference store — for
recurring queries (e.g., "My active WOs", "Late WOs", "Press
Shop today"). Persistence must support save, recall, update, and
delete against the current user.
preconditions: At least 50 WOs exist across statuses, work centers, and due dates.

steps.action:
Configure: filter to status = In-progress + work center =
Press Shop, sort by due date ascending.
steps.expected: List reflects the configuration.

steps.action:
Persist the configuration under a name / identifier (e.g.,
"Press Shop active") via whatever mechanism the application
provides — dedicated saved-views menu, or a generic
user-preferences key/value entry (e.g., POST to
/user-preferences).

Switch to a default list view, then recall the persisted
configuration via the same mechanism used in step 2.
steps.expected: Recalled configuration restores the exact filter / sort.

steps.action:
Update the persisted configuration: change one filter and
save in place under the same identifier.
steps.expected: Stored configuration updates.

steps.action: Delete the persisted configuration.
steps.expected:
Entry removed from the persistence layer (menu or key/value
store). Default list unaffected.

expected_overall:
Persisted table state supports recurring production-floor
workflows regardless of whether the application surfaces it as
a first-class saved-views UI or as a generic user-preferences
key/value store.
pass_criteria:
Persist, recall, update, and delete of table state all work
cleanly. Either a dedicated UI affordance OR a generic key/value
preference store satisfies this requirement.
notes:
Reconciled in Phase 2 — when the application develops a
dedicated saved-views UI, this case will tighten to require
that surface.

title: WO list exports the currently visible rows and columns
goal:
Verify the WO list export honors filter, sort, and column
visibility, and that computed columns (% complete, variance)
export as numbers.
preconditions: At least 100 WOs exist across statuses with varied % complete.

steps.action:
Filter to status = In-progress. Sort by due date ascending.
Hide one default column and show % complete.
steps.expected: On-screen view reflects all settings.

steps.action: Trigger CSV export.
steps.expected: File downloads with sensible name.

steps.action:
Open in spreadsheet tool. Verify row count, column set, sort
order match the on-screen view.
steps.expected:
Match. % complete exports as a number (e.g., 0.45 or 45), not
a string with a percent sign.

steps.action: Verify date columns export as dates and are sortable as dates.
steps.expected: Dates typed correctly.

expected_overall: WO export is suitable for production reporting downstream.
pass_criteria: Filter / sort / columns honored. Computed and date types preserved.

title: Pending approval that's been waiting beyond a threshold reminds the approver
goal:
Verify that a pending approval (e.g., requisition, PO, ECR, JE)
that's been waiting beyond a documented threshold fires a reminder
to the approver.
preconditions: At least one approval workflow exists with a documented reminder threshold (e.g., 24 hours).

steps.action:
Submit a requisition (or ECR / JE) requiring approval. Wait
past the threshold (or backdate the submission).
steps.expected: A reminder notification fires for the approver.

steps.action: Approve or reject.
steps.expected: Reminder clears.

expected_overall: Approval reminders nudge approvers without spamming them.
pass_criteria: Reminder fires past threshold AND clears on action AND not duplicated.

title: Approval still pending past escalation threshold escalates to next-level approver
goal:
Verify that a pending approval ignored beyond a documented escalation
threshold (e.g., 48 hours past first reminder) escalates to the next
approver in the chain or to a backup approver.
preconditions: An approval workflow exists with escalation policy and a defined next-level approver.

steps.action:
Submit a request requiring approval. Backdate (or wait) past the
first reminder threshold.
steps.expected: First reminder fires for the primary approver.

steps.action: Continue past the escalation threshold without approver action.
steps.expected: Escalation notification fires for the next-level approver.

steps.action: The next-level approver acts (approve or reject).
steps.expected: All reminders and escalations clear. Audit shows the path.

expected_overall: Escalation prevents requests from sitting indefinitely.
pass_criteria:
First reminder AND escalation both fire at correct thresholds AND
clear on action AND audit trail is complete.

title: Approval reminders route to the delegate when primary approver is on delegation
goal:
Verify that when the primary approver has an active delegation
configured (e.g., out-of-office), reminders for pending approvals
route to the delegate, not the primary, for the duration of the
delegation window.
preconditions: An approval workflow exists.
Primary approver has an active delegation set to a backup user.

steps.action:
Submit a request requiring approval while the delegation window
is active.
steps.expected:
The delegate receives the initial approval notification. The
primary does not (or receives a copy only if policy says so).

steps.action: Wait past the reminder threshold without action.
steps.expected: The reminder fires to the delegate, not the primary.

steps.action: End the delegation window. Submit a new request.
steps.expected: The new request and its reminders go to the primary.

expected_overall: Delegation correctly redirects routing for the duration of the window.
pass_criteria:
Delegate receives reminders during window AND primary receives them
outside of window AND audit records the delegation routing.

title: AR aging triggers fire at documented aging buckets
goal:
Verify that when an invoice crosses an aging bucket boundary
(e.g., enters 31-60 days past due), a notification fires to the
collections / controller team, surfacing the invoice for action.
preconditions: At least one customer invoice exists.

steps.action:
Backdate (or wait) so an existing invoice crosses into the
31-60 day past-due bucket.
steps.expected: An AR aging notification fires.

steps.action: Apply payment to the invoice.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

expected_overall: Aging-bucket alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria: Alert fires at bucket transition AND clears on payment.

title: AR aging fires fresh notifications at each bucket transition (60-90, 90+)
goal:
Verify that an unpaid invoice triggers a separate notification each
time it crosses into a deeper aging bucket (31-60, 61-90, 90+), and
that each escalation includes the appropriate recipients (e.g., 90+
copies the controller and credit officer).
preconditions: An unpaid customer invoice exists, old enough to walk through multiple buckets via backdating.

steps.action: Backdate the invoice into the 31-60 bucket.
steps.expected: A notification fires to collections / account manager.

steps.action: Backdate further into the 61-90 bucket.
steps.expected: A new notification fires; recipient list expands per policy.

steps.action: Backdate into the 90+ bucket.
steps.expected:
A new notification fires; controller and credit officer are
included.

steps.action: Receive payment in full.
steps.expected: Active alerts clear across all buckets.

expected_overall: Each bucket transition produces a fresh, correctly-routed alert.
pass_criteria:
Each transition fires once AND recipients widen per policy AND payment
clears all alerts.

title: AR aging digest delivers one summary per recipient on the documented schedule
goal:
Verify that recipients subscribed to the AR aging digest (e.g., daily
or weekly summary) receive one consolidated email listing all their
in-scope overdue invoices, instead of one notification per invoice.
preconditions: At least one customer has multiple invoices in past-due buckets assigned to a recipient configured for digest mode.

steps.action:
Confirm the recipient is in digest mode. Trigger or wait for the
next scheduled digest.
steps.expected:
The recipient receives one summary email listing each overdue
invoice with bucket, amount, and customer.

steps.action:
Apply payment to one of the listed invoices. Trigger the next
digest.
steps.expected:
The paid invoice is no longer in the digest. Remaining invoices
still listed correctly.

steps.action:
Switch the recipient to per-event mode and back. Verify next
delivery matches the chosen mode.
steps.expected:
Per-event mode delivers individual notifications; digest mode
consolidates.

expected_overall: Digest mode is honored end-to-end for AR aging.
pass_criteria:
Digest contains all in-scope invoices AND excludes paid AND mode
switches take effect on the next delivery.

title: Sales order that pushes a customer over their credit limit alerts sales and credit
goal:
Verify that when a sales order (or order edit) pushes a customer's
total exposure above their configured credit limit, a notification
fires to the sales rep and credit officer, surfacing the order for
review or hold.
preconditions: A customer has a configured credit limit and existing exposure (open orders + AR balance) close to the limit.

steps.action:
Enter (or edit) a sales order whose total brings exposure above
the credit limit.
steps.expected:
A credit-limit exceeded notification fires to the sales rep and
credit officer. Order is flagged or placed on credit hold per
policy.

steps.action:
Reduce order quantity / amount so exposure falls back under the
limit.
steps.expected: Alert clears. Hold lifts per policy.

steps.action: Enter a second order that again exceeds the limit.
steps.expected:
A fresh notification fires (no stuck or duplicated alerts from
the prior cycle).

expected_overall: Credit-limit alert fires precisely when exposure crosses the limit.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires on exceedance AND clears on remediation AND repeats
cleanly on subsequent breaches.

title: Releasing a credit-held shipment notifies controller with override audit
goal:
Verify that releasing a shipment for a customer currently over their
credit limit (manual override) fires a notification to the controller
and records the override in the customer's audit trail.
preconditions: A sales order is on credit hold for a customer over their credit limit.

steps.action:
As an authorized user, override the credit hold and release the
shipment.
steps.expected:
A notification fires to the controller naming the order, customer,
override actor, and reason if captured.

steps.action: Open the customer audit trail.
steps.expected:
The override is logged with who / when / amount-over-limit /
reason.

steps.action: Attempt the same override as a user without permission.
steps.expected:
Override is blocked; no notification or audit entry is generated
for the failed attempt beyond a permission-denied log entry.

expected_overall: Credit overrides are visible to finance leadership and fully audited.
pass_criteria:
Override notification fires AND audit captures full detail AND
unauthorized attempts are blocked and logged separately.

title: Accounting sync failure fires alert with error details to integrations owner
goal:
Verify that when a scheduled accounting / GL sync run fails (auth
error, transport error, payload reject), an alert fires to the
integrations owner and to the controller, with enough detail to
triage (run id, timestamp, error code or message, affected record
count if available).
preconditions: An accounting integration is configured and scheduled.
A failure can be induced (invalid credential, malformed payload, or destination unreachable).

steps.action:
Induce a sync failure (invalid credential or simulated
destination outage). Trigger or wait for the next sync run.
steps.expected:
An integration-failure notification fires to the integrations
owner and controller with run id, timestamp, and error detail.

steps.action: Restore credentials / connectivity. Re-run the sync.
steps.expected:
Sync succeeds. A "recovered" notification (or silent clear of
the prior alert) fires per policy.

steps.action: Open the integration log.
steps.expected: Both the failure and the recovery are recorded with full detail.

expected_overall:
Sync failures surface immediately to the right people with enough
detail to act.
pass_criteria:
Failure alert fires with actionable detail AND recovery is
acknowledged AND log captures both events.

title: Rejected inbound EDI document (e.g., 850, 856) alerts EDI coordinator with document detail
goal:
Verify that when an inbound EDI document fails validation (schema
error, partner mapping error, missing reference), an alert fires to
the EDI coordinator and the relevant business owner (e.g., customer
service for 850, receiving for 856), naming the trading partner,
document type, control number, and rejection reason.
preconditions: EDI integration is configured for at least one trading partner with a documented document-type mapping.

steps.action:
Submit an inbound EDI document that fails validation (malformed,
unknown partner reference, or invalid item).
steps.expected:
An alert fires to the EDI coordinator and the document-type
business owner with partner, document type, control number, and
reason.

steps.action: Resubmit a corrected version.
steps.expected: Document accepts. The prior alert clears or is marked resolved.

steps.action: Open the EDI log.
steps.expected: Both rejection and acceptance are recorded with control numbers.

expected_overall:
EDI rejections do not silently disappear; they reach the right team
with traceable detail.
pass_criteria:
Rejection alert fires with full document detail AND recovery is
recorded AND business owner routing is correct per document type.

title: Low-stock alert fires when on-hand drops below reorder point
goal:
Verify that when on-hand for a part drops below its reorder point,
a notification fires to the configured recipient(s), and that the
notification clears once stock is replenished above the point.
preconditions: A part has reorder point and notification subscribers configured (P3-SS-001).

steps.action: Issue or adjust on-hand to drop below the reorder point.
steps.expected:
A low-stock notification fires within the documented window
(in-app and / or email per channel config).

steps.action: Receive new stock to bring on-hand back above reorder point.
steps.expected: The active alert clears (or a "resolved" indicator appears).

steps.action:
Repeat the cycle to verify it doesn't fire-and-stick after
multiple cycles.
steps.expected: Each crossing fires fresh; resolved alerts don't pile up.

expected_overall: Low-stock alert fires and clears predictably.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires on threshold breach AND clears on recovery AND no
duplicate / stuck alerts.

title: Low-stock alert honors per-user channel preferences (in-app vs email)
goal:
Verify that subscribers who have disabled the email channel for
low-stock alerts still receive the in-app notification, and that
subscribers who have disabled in-app still receive email — neither
channel fires for a user who has opted out of both.
preconditions: A part has reorder point and at least three subscribers configured with distinct channel preferences (email-only, in-app-only, both disabled).

steps.action: Drop on-hand below reorder point.
steps.expected:
The email-only subscriber receives email and no in-app banner.
The in-app-only subscriber sees the banner and no email.
The fully-opted-out subscriber receives nothing on either channel.

steps.action:
Toggle the opted-out subscriber's preferences to enable in-app,
then trigger another threshold breach (recover and re-breach).
steps.expected: That subscriber now receives the in-app alert on the next breach.

expected_overall: Per-user preferences are authoritative for channel delivery.
pass_criteria:
Each subscriber receives only on their enabled channel(s) AND
preference changes take effect on the next firing.

title: Low-stock alert fires per location when a multi-location part dips at one site
goal:
Verify that for a part stocked at multiple locations with per-location
reorder points, an alert fires for the specific location that breaches
— not aggregated across the network — and the alert names the location.
preconditions: A part is stocked at two or more locations, each with its own reorder point and subscriber list.

steps.action:
Drop on-hand at Location A below A's reorder point while
Location B remains well above its point.
steps.expected: A low-stock alert fires naming Location A. No alert for Location B.

steps.action:
Open the alert and confirm the location, on-hand, and reorder
point shown.
steps.expected: Location, on-hand, and reorder point match Location A's actuals.

steps.action:
Replenish Location A above its point. Then drop Location B below
its point.
steps.expected: Location A alert clears. A new alert fires naming Location B.

expected_overall: Alerts are scoped to the specific location that breached.
pass_criteria:
Each alert names the correct location AND does not fire for unaffected
locations AND clears independently per location.

title: Period close pending alert lists subledgers with open items blocking close
goal:
Verify that when a fiscal period is being closed and subledgers
(AP, AR, inventory, payroll) still have unposted or in-flight items,
a notification fires to the controller listing each subledger and
the count or types of blocking items.
preconditions: A fiscal period is approaching its close date.
At least two subledgers contain open items (e.g., unposted AP invoices, uninvoiced shipments).

steps.action:
Initiate the close-readiness check (or wait for the scheduled
pre-close notification window).
steps.expected:
A notification fires to the controller listing each blocking
subledger and the open item types. Subledger owners (AP / AR
clerks) receive their specific portions.

steps.action: Resolve all open AP items but leave AR open.
steps.expected: The next notification reflects only AR as blocking.

steps.action: Resolve remaining items.
steps.expected: Notification clears or transitions to a "ready to close" state.

expected_overall: Pre-close notifications give finance a clear, accurate punchlist.
pass_criteria:
Notification lists exactly the open items AND updates as items are
resolved AND clears when none remain.

title: Period-close countdown reminders fire on documented schedule before deadline
goal:
Verify that pre-close reminders fire to the close team on the
documented cadence (e.g., 5 / 3 / 1 days before close deadline) and
that final-day reminders escalate to the controller.
preconditions: A fiscal period has a configured close deadline and reminder cadence.

steps.action: Backdate (or wait) so the system is 5 days before close deadline.
steps.expected: First reminder fires to the close team.

steps.action: Advance to 3 days before deadline, then 1 day before.
steps.expected: Each scheduled reminder fires once (no duplicates) on its day.

steps.action: Advance to deadline day with the period still open.
steps.expected: Final-day reminder escalates to the controller.

steps.action: Close the period.
steps.expected: No further reminders fire for this period.

expected_overall: Close-deadline reminders fire predictably and stop on close.
pass_criteria:
Each scheduled reminder fires exactly once AND escalation lands on
deadline day AND reminders cease after close.

title: PM that's past its scheduled date triggers an overdue alert
goal:
Verify a scheduled PM that has not been started by its scheduled
date fires an alert to the maintenance manager and asset owner.
preconditions: A PM is scheduled with a date in the past (or backdate to make it past due).

steps.action: Configure or backdate a PM to be past its scheduled start.
steps.expected: An overdue alert fires for the maintenance manager.

steps.action: Start the PM.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

expected_overall: Overdue PM alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria: Alert fires when overdue AND clears on PM start.

title: PM still not started past escalation threshold escalates to plant manager
goal:
Verify that an overdue PM that has not been started past a documented
secondary threshold (e.g., 5 days past due) escalates to the plant
manager in addition to maintenance manager.
preconditions: A scheduled PM exists with escalation policy defined.

steps.action:
Backdate the PM so it has been overdue beyond the secondary
escalation threshold without start.
steps.expected:
The plant manager receives an escalation. Maintenance manager's
original alert continues to show in their queue.

steps.action: Start and complete the PM.
steps.expected: Both the original alert and the escalation clear.

expected_overall: Escalation enforces accountability for chronically overdue PMs.
pass_criteria:
Escalation fires only past the secondary threshold AND clears with
PM completion AND audit trail records both alerts.

title: Overdue PM notifies the asset owner per asset notification config
goal:
Verify that when an asset has a designated owner / cost-center
contact, an overdue PM on that asset notifies that owner in addition
to maintenance staff.
preconditions: An asset has an owner / cost-center contact configured.
A PM is scheduled against that asset.

steps.action:
Backdate the PM so it is past due. Confirm the asset has its
owner contact set.
steps.expected:
The maintenance manager and the asset owner both receive the
overdue alert.

steps.action: Reassign the asset owner to a different user.
steps.expected:
Subsequent overdue notifications go to the new owner; prior alert
history stays attributed to the previous owner.

expected_overall: Asset ownership drives correct alert routing for PMs.
pass_criteria:
Asset owner is included in routing AND owner reassignment redirects
future alerts AND prior alert history is preserved.

title: PO past expected delivery date triggers an overdue alert
goal:
Verify that an issued PO whose expected delivery date has passed
without full receipt fires an overdue alert to the buyer and / or
buyer team.
preconditions: At least one issued PO with an expected delivery date in the past and not fully received.

steps.action:
Issue (or backdate) a PO so its expected delivery date is in
the past with no receipts.
steps.expected: An overdue notification fires for the buyer.

steps.action: Receive the PO fully.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

steps.action: Open the PO. Verify the alert history is auditable.
steps.expected: History shows the alert fired and resolved.

expected_overall: Overdue PO alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria:
Alert present after due date AND cleared on receipt AND history
recorded.

title: PO with outstanding balance past expected delivery still triggers overdue alert
goal:
Verify a partially-received PO whose remaining open quantity is past
the expected delivery date still fires an overdue alert. The alert
references the open balance, not the original ordered quantity.
preconditions: An issued PO has been partially received and has an open balance, with the expected delivery date in the past.

steps.action:
Receive partial quantity against a PO. Backdate (or wait) so the
expected delivery date is in the past with quantity still open.
steps.expected:
An overdue notification fires referencing the open balance
(not the full ordered qty).

steps.action: Receive the remaining balance.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

steps.action: Open the PO history.
steps.expected: History records both partial receipt and the alert lifecycle.

expected_overall: Partial receipts do not silence the overdue alert while balance remains.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires while balance is open past due AND clears only on full
receipt AND alert content reflects open balance.

title: PO that remains overdue past escalation threshold notifies procurement manager
goal:
Verify that a PO still open beyond a documented escalation window
(e.g., 7 days past expected delivery) escalates to the procurement
manager in addition to the buyer.
preconditions: A PO has been overdue past the documented escalation window with no receipt activity. Escalation policy is configured.

steps.action: Backdate (or wait) so a PO is overdue beyond the escalation window.
steps.expected:
The procurement manager receives an escalation notification in
addition to the buyer's standing overdue alert.

steps.action: Receive the PO fully.
steps.expected: Both the buyer alert and the manager escalation clear.

steps.action: Inspect the PO audit trail.
steps.expected: Audit shows the original alert, the escalation, and the resolution.

expected_overall:
Escalation kicks in only when the situation has stalled past the
documented threshold.
pass_criteria:
Manager escalation fires only after the threshold AND not before AND
audit trail captures both events.

title: Part hitting zero on-hand with open demand fires a stockout alert
goal:
Verify that when a part's on-hand drops to zero while open demand
(sales orders, work orders, transfers) still references it, a
stockout alert fires — distinct from low-stock — to procurement
and to demand owners (sales, production planner).
preconditions: A part has open demand (at least one sales order or work order requiring it) and on-hand can be driven to zero.

steps.action: Issue or adjust on-hand to zero while demand remains open.
steps.expected:
A stockout alert fires (distinct from any low-stock alert) to
procurement and demand owners. The alert references at least one
affected order.

steps.action: Receive stock to bring on-hand above zero.
steps.expected:
Stockout alert clears. (Low-stock alert may remain if still under
reorder point.)

steps.action: Drive on-hand back to zero with no open demand on the part.
steps.expected: No stockout alert fires (because there's nothing being blocked).

expected_overall:
Stockout alert is reserved for the case that on-hand zero is
blocking real demand.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires when zero-on-hand blocks demand AND clears on receipt
AND does not fire when no demand is impacted.

title: Component stockout that blocks an in-progress WO alerts production with WO context
goal:
Verify that a component reaching zero on-hand while a work order
needs it for an upcoming operation (or is currently waiting on it)
alerts production with the affected WO id, operation, and required
quantity.
preconditions: An in-progress work order requires a specific component for an upcoming or current operation.

steps.action:
Drive the component's on-hand to zero (issue to another WO or
adjust).
steps.expected:
A stockout alert fires to production with WO id, operation, and
required quantity. Procurement also receives the alert.

steps.action: Receive enough component to satisfy the WO's need.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

steps.action: Inspect the WO record.
steps.expected: The WO shows a history entry for the stockout incident.

expected_overall:
Stockouts impacting active manufacturing are surfaced with enough
detail to act on the right WO.
pass_criteria:
Alert names the affected WO and operation AND clears on receipt AND
WO history records the incident.

title: Work order operation past its planned end alerts production supervisor
goal:
Verify that when an in-progress operation on a work order passes
its planned end date / time without completion, a notification fires
to the production supervisor and the responsible work center owner.
preconditions: A work order is in progress with a routed operation that has a planned end date in the past (or can be backdated).

steps.action:
Backdate (or wait so) an in-progress operation's planned end is
now in the past with the operation not yet complete.
steps.expected:
An alert fires to the production supervisor and the work center
owner naming the WO, operation, planned end, and elapsed slip.

steps.action: Complete the operation.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

steps.action: Inspect the WO history.
steps.expected: Slip event and resolution are recorded.

expected_overall: Slip alerts give shop-floor leadership timely visibility into late ops.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires past planned end AND clears on completion AND history
reflects the slip.

title: Cumulative slip projecting WO to miss its due date escalates to planner and customer-facing role
goal:
Verify that when cumulative operation slip pushes a work order's
projected completion past its scheduled due date, an escalation alert
fires to the production planner. If the WO is tied to a sales order
with a customer commitment, the account manager is also notified.
preconditions: A work order has multiple routed operations and a due date.
At least one earlier operation has slipped enough that the projected completion now exceeds the WO due date.
Optionally, the WO is linked to a sales order with a promised ship date.

steps.action:
Backdate or extend prior operations so the WO is now projected
to finish past its due date.
steps.expected:
Escalation alert fires to the production planner. If linked to a
sales order, the account manager also receives a notification.

steps.action: Catch up later operations so the WO can finish on time.
steps.expected: Escalation clears (or transitions to "back on track").

steps.action: Re-slip and recover again.
steps.expected: Each cycle produces a fresh, non-stuck escalation.

expected_overall:
Forward-looking slip detection reaches both production and customer-
facing roles when commitments are at risk.
pass_criteria:
Escalation fires when projected end exceeds due date AND clears on
recovery AND reaches the account manager only when a customer
commitment exists.

title: Create the first administrator account
goal:
Establish the first user. This person will configure the rest of
the company.
preconditions: You completed P0-INSTALL-001.
No user accounts exist yet.

steps.action:
From the starting page, follow the path that begins setup or
creates the first account.
steps.expected:
A form appears asking for information about the person setting
up the company. At minimum: name, email address, and a password
(or equivalent way to secure the account).

steps.action:
Fill in the form with these values:
  Name: Alex Morgan
  Email: alex.morgan@cascade-components.example
  Password: a strong password of your choice (write it down —
  you will need it)
Submit the form.
steps.expected:
The form accepts the submission. You are either signed in
automatically or directed to sign in with the credentials you
just created.

steps.action:
If you were not automatically signed in, sign in using the email
and password from step 2.
steps.expected:
You are signed in as Alex Morgan. The application shows it
recognizes you (your name, initials, or email is visible
somewhere on the page).

expected_overall:
An administrator account exists and is signed in. The system
recognizes this person as the first user.
pass_criteria:
After completing all steps, you are signed in to the application
with the account you just created.
why_this_matters:
This is the moment the company becomes a real tenant in the
application. Anything that goes wrong here blocks all further setup.

negative_variants.title: Reject obviously invalid email
negative_variants.action: In the email field, enter "not-an-email" and try to submit.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission. A message in plain English explains
that the email address is not valid. The message does not say
anything like "regex match failed" or "validation error code 7."
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Submission was blocked AND the message was understandable to a
non-technical person.

negative_variants.title: Reject empty required fields
negative_variants.action: Leave one of the required fields empty and try to submit.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission. The message identifies which field
is missing, in plain language.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Submission was blocked AND the missing field was clearly
identified.

negative_variants.title: Reject weak password (if password rules apply)
negative_variants.action: Try to submit with the password "password" or "12345."
negative_variants.expected:
Either the form blocks submission with a clear explanation of
what's wrong with the password, OR the application accepts it
but warns that the password is weak. Both behaviors are
acceptable; what is NOT acceptable is silently accepting "12345"
with no comment.
